text: The Nathanson's liver retractor lifts the liver and falciform ligament, revealing a larger-than-expected gastric sleeve. The surgeon dissects the duodenum above and below, moving behind it, with visible adhesions in the omentum and the pancreas located posteriorly. Approximately four centimeters of duodenum is dissected from the duodenogastric junction.

The surgeon uses a blunt dissector to carefully dissect the duodenum to the right of the common bile duct (CBD) and vessels, ensuring not to injure surrounding structures. The pancreas is visible just behind the duodenum. The dissection creates space for the introduction of a GAA gun, and a tan tri-staple stapler is used.

After dividing the duodenum, a swab is placed at the edges to check for bile leakage and assist with hemostasis. The surgeon measures the ileum starting from the ileocecal junction, with the appendix visible nearby, extending approximately 200 to 300 centimeters. This segment of the ileum is then anastomosed to the proximal duodenum.

The surgeon uses two or three VLOC PDS sutures to perform a two-layer posterior and anterior closure along the staple line, securing the sutures with a stay suture at the end. The duodenum and ileum are opened using either a harmonic or hook, and the anastomosis begins with a 2-0 V-lock suture at the opened edges.

The surgeon continues suturing along the opened edges, invaginating the tissue by placing sutures from right to left, facilitating the anastomosis. The suturing extends beyond the opening to encompass the entire anastomosis area within the suture line. An orogastric tube may be inserted through the anastomosis.

The surgeon locks the last two sutures, continuing the first posterior layer suture as the fourth layer anteriorly to prevent leaks. The sutures are through and through, ensuring the lumen remains open. The cut end of the duodenum appears very vascular with a normal color, indicating healthy tissue.

The surgeon transitions from a single anastomosis duodenal switch to a Roux-en-Y configuration. The alimentary limb is opened approximately 250 centimeters from the ileocecal junction, creating a 150-centimeter common channel. The enterotomy is closed using a V-Loc 2-0 suture. The biliary pancreatic limb is disconnected from the duodenoileal anastomosis, proximal to the ileoileal anastomosis, with submucosal stitches ensuring proper edge approximation and healing.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect at the ileo-ileal anastomosis using non-absorbable V-lock sutures, starting with the visible 'V' shape and continuing with side-to-side sutures to approximate the mesenteries. The Peterson space is exposed by lifting the transverse colon and ileum, and the mesentery and transverse mesocolon are approximated using the same sutures.
The surgeon positions the omentum down into the surgical space and inserts a drain into the right subhepatic space.
